lesson.title: Backtesting
lesson.body: Backtesting simulates a strategy on historical data to estimate performance. Pitfalls include: overfitting (too many parameters optimized in-sample), look-ahead bias (using data not available at decision time), survivorship bias (using only stocks that survived), and data snooping. Walk-forward validation splits data into rolling IS and OOS windows. A strategy that doesn't work OOS is likely overfit.

rooms.question: Look-ahead bias in backtesting means:
answers: Using information at time t that was only available after time t
Looking too far back in history
rooms.explanation: Look-ahead bias = data snooping in time: e.g., using quarterly earnings announced on day T to make a decision on day T-1. This inflates backtest performance unrealistically.

rooms.question: Survivorship bias in stock backtesting occurs when you:
answers: Only test on stocks that currently exist, ignoring delisted/bankrupt firms
Use only the best-performing historical period
rooms.explanation: Survivorship bias: historical universes include companies that went bankrupt or were delisted. Ignoring them inflates apparent strategy returns (you only see stocks that survived).

rooms.question: A strategy shows a Sharpe of 2.1 in-sample and 0.3 out-of-sample. This indicates:
answers: Severe overfitting — the strategy was fit to noise, not signal
A good strategy with some OOS variation
The OOS period is just unlucky
rooms.explanation: IS/OOS Sharpe collapse from 2.1 to 0.3 is a strong sign of overfitting. A legitimate strategy degrades moderately OOS, not catastrophically.

rooms.question: Order the steps of walk-forward validation:
items: Advance the window and repeat; aggregate OOS results
rooms.explanation: Walk-forward: fix IS window, optimize, test OOS, slide the window forward. Repeat across multiple IS/OOS pairs for a realistic OOS track record.

rooms.question: The Deflated Sharpe Ratio (Bailey & Lopez de Prado) adjusts the Sharpe ratio for:
answers: Multiple testing and backtest length (more trials → lower significance)
rooms.explanation: If you test 100 strategies, some will look good by chance. The Deflated SR penalizes for the number of trials and non-normality, giving a probability that the SR is genuinely positive.

boss.name: The Overfit Model
boss.taunt: I have 50 parameters and 100 data points. I fit perfectly. I predict nothing.

questions.question: The minimum backtest length to achieve a given Sharpe ratio with statistical significance grows:
answers: Quadratically with the inverse Sharpe ratio (lower SR needs much longer backtest)
Does not depend on Sharpe ratio
questions.explanation: Required observations n ≈ (Sharpe ratio / 0.5)⁻² · z². For SR=0.5, need ~4 years of monthly data; for SR=2.0, only ~1 year.

questions.question: Transaction costs in backtesting should include:
answers: Commission, bid-ask spread, market impact, and short-selling costs
questions.explanation: Full transaction cost model: commissions + half the bid-ask spread + market impact (especially for larger positions). Ignoring impact is the most common backtest error.

questions.question: The primary defense against data snooping bias is:
answers: Holding out a genuine OOS period that is never touched during development
questions.explanation: A 'pure' hold-out set, never seen during development, is the gold standard. Once you've looked at OOS results and adjusted, it's no longer truly OOS.
